events.title: Spanish-American War: US Occupies Cuba
events.detail: Following the sinking of the USS Maine in Havana harbor, the US declared war on Spain on April 25, 1898. US forces defeated Spanish troops within months, and the Treaty of Paris (December 1898) transferred Cuba, Puerto Rico, Guam, and the Philippines to US control. Cuba formally came under US military occupation until 1902, though US influence persisted through the Platt Amendment.

sources.name: Library of Congress: Spanish-American War
Foreign Affairs: US Imperial Moment

events.title: Puerto Rico Annexed by United States
events.detail: US forces landed in Puerto Rico on July 25, 1898 during the Spanish-American War. The Treaty of Paris ceded Puerto Rico to the US as an unincorporated territory. Puerto Ricans were denied full US citizenship until 1917 and remain unable to vote in presidential elections to this day.

sources.name: US National Archives: Treaty of Paris 1898
New York Times historical archive

media.source: Wikipedia / List of United States cities by population

events.title: Platt Amendment: Cuba as US Protectorate
events.detail: Congress passed the Platt Amendment in 1901, forcing Cuba to incorporate its terms into its own constitution as a condition of US military withdrawal. The amendment gave the US the right to intervene in Cuban affairs, limited Cuba's ability to sign treaties or incur debt, and established Guantánamo Bay as a permanent US naval base. Cuba became a de facto US protectorate.

sources.name: US State Department Historical Archive
AP Historical Archive

events.title: Panama Canal Zone: Hay-Bunau-Varilla Treaty
events.detail: After Colombia refused to ratify a canal treaty, the US backed a Panamanian independence movement. On November 3, 1903, Panama declared independence; the US recognized Panama within three days and dispatched warships to prevent Colombian forces from suppressing the revolt. The Hay-Bunau-Varilla Treaty of November 18, 1903 granted the US a 10-mile-wide Canal Zone 'in perpetuity,' for which it paid $10 million.

sources.name: US State Department FOIA: Panama Canal negotiations
Reuters Historical Archive

events.title: Roosevelt Corollary: US Claims Right to Intervene in LatAm
events.detail: President Theodore Roosevelt's 1904 Corollary to the Monroe Doctrine asserted that the US had the right and duty to intervene in Latin American nations unable to maintain order or meet their international obligations. This doctrine justified numerous US interventions over the following decades. It was used to justify the Dominican customs receivership (1905), Nicaraguan occupation, and Haitian occupation.

sources.name: US Congressional Record 1904
Foreign Affairs: Monroe Doctrine Legacy

events.title: Second US Occupation of Cuba (1906–1909)
events.detail: After a disputed Cuban presidential election led to armed revolt, President Roosevelt ordered a second US military occupation of Cuba under the Platt Amendment. Secretary of War William Howard Taft administered Cuba as provisional governor. The US occupation lasted until 1909, when new elections were held under US supervision.

sources.name: US War Department records 1906
AP Historical Archive

events.title: US Marines Occupy Nicaragua (1912–1925)
events.detail: The US deployed Marines to Nicaragua in August 1912 to protect the conservative government and US financial interests. A small 'legation guard' of Marines remained until 1925, effectively controlling Nicaraguan finances and security. The US forced Nicaragua to sign the Bryan-Chamorro Treaty (1914) giving the US exclusive rights to a Nicaraguan canal route and naval bases.

sources.name: US Marine Corps Historical Division
NACLA: US Intervention Nicaragua

events.title: US Occupation of Veracruz, Mexico
events.detail: President Wilson ordered US forces to occupy the Mexican port of Veracruz on April 21, 1914, ostensibly after a minor diplomatic incident (the 'Tampico Affair'), but with the goal of preventing a German arms shipment to the Huerta regime and undermining the Mexican government. The occupation lasted seven months, killing 19 Americans and 126–172 Mexicans. It deepened anti-US sentiment throughout Latin America.

sources.name: US Navy Historical Records 1914
New York Times 1914 archive

events.title: US Occupation of Haiti (1915–1934)
events.detail: Following the assassination of Haitian President Vilbrun Guillaume Sam, the US Marines landed in Haiti on July 28, 1915. The US forced Haiti to accept a treaty making it a protectorate, controlled its customs, finances, and military. The occupation lasted 19 years. Haitian resistance movements (Cacos) were suppressed with significant loss of life—estimates of Haitian dead range from 2,000 to 15,000.

sources.name: US Marine Corps Historical Division
HRW: Haiti Historical Abuses

media.source: Wikipedia / United States occupation of Haiti

events.title: US Military Occupation of Dominican Republic (1916–1924)
events.detail: US Marines occupied the Dominican Republic from May 1916 to September 1924, establishing a military government that disarmed the population, controlled finances, and suppressed nationalist resistance. The Guardia Nacional, trained by US Marines, became the power base for the future dictator Rafael Trujillo, who would go on to rule for 30 years with tacit US support.

sources.name: US State Department Historical Archive
NACLA Historical Report

events.title: US Returns to Nicaragua; Sandino Guerrilla War
events.detail: US Marines returned to Nicaragua in 1927 after civil conflict. Augusto César Sandino refused to accept the US-brokered Peace of Tipitapa and launched a guerrilla war against occupation forces. The US military struggled against Sandino's forces until withdrawing in 1933. The US left behind the Guardia Nacional under Anastasio Somoza García, who assassinated Sandino in 1934 and established a 40-year family dictatorship.

sources.name: US Marine Corps Historical Division
National Security Archive: Nicaragua

events.title: Banana Wars: United Fruit Company & US Military
events.detail: Across Central America and the Caribbean, the US repeatedly intervened militarily to protect corporate interests—particularly United Fruit Company (later Chiquita) and Standard Fruit. Honduras experienced so many US interventions on behalf of banana companies that journalist O. Henry coined the term 'banana republic.' US troops intervened in Honduras in 1903, 1907, 1911, 1912, 1919, 1924, and 1925.

sources.name: Smedley Butler: War is a Racket (1935)
NACLA: Banana Wars historical analysis

events.title: Gen. Smedley Butler: 'War Is a Racket'
events.detail: Retired Marine Major General Smedley Butler published 'War Is a Racket' in 1935, confessing he had served as a 'muscle man' for US corporations in Latin America. He wrote: 'I helped make Haiti and Cuba a decent place for the National City Bank to collect revenues...I helped purify Nicaragua for the international banking house of Brown Brothers.' His account remains a foundational critique of US interventionism.

sources.name: Smedley Butler: War is a Racket, Round Table Press 1935
Common Sense Magazine 1935

era: Good Neighbor Policy & Cold War Origins (1933–1953)

events.title: FDR's Good Neighbor Policy Announced
events.detail: President Franklin D. Roosevelt announced the Good Neighbor Policy in his 1933 inaugural address, pledging to be a 'good neighbor' and renouncing the right to unilateral US intervention in Latin American affairs. The US abrogated the Platt Amendment (1934) and withdrew from Haiti (1934). However, the policy was largely rhetorical; the US continued to back favorable dictators throughout the region.

sources.name: FDR Inaugural Address, US National Archives 1933
Foreign Affairs: FDR and Latin America

events.title: Platt Amendment Abrogated; Guantánamo Retained
events.detail: The US abrogated the Platt Amendment through the Cuban-American Treaty of 1934, removing the formal right to intervene in Cuban affairs—but retained the Guantánamo Bay Naval Station under a lease that could only be terminated by mutual consent. The base has remained under US control ever since, becoming a source of ongoing controversy.

sources.name: US-Cuba Treaty of 1934, US State Department
Reuters Historical Archive

events.title: US Supports Trujillo Dictatorship in Dominican Republic
events.detail: Rafael Trujillo, trained by US Marines during the 1916–1924 occupation, rose to power in 1930 and ruled with brutal repression until 1961. The US repeatedly backed his regime as a 'stable' anti-communist partner, overlooking the 1937 Parsley Massacre of 20,000 Haitian migrants and systematic torture of political prisoners. FDR allegedly said of Trujillo: 'He may be a son of a bitch, but he's our son of a bitch.'

sources.name: National Security Archive: Dominican Republic declassified files
New York Times Historical Archive

media.source: Wikipedia / History of the Dominican Republic

events.title: Guatemalan Revolution: Democracy Arrives
events.detail: A popular revolution in October 1944 overthrew the US-backed dictator Jorge Ubico and brought democratic elections to Guatemala. Juan José Arévalo won the presidency and implemented social reforms. His successor, Jacobo Árbenz (elected 1951), continued reforms including land redistribution targeting United Fruit Company holdings. These democratic governments would be targeted by CIA covert operations within a decade.

sources.name: US State Department Guatemala Declassified 1944
National Security Archive: Guatemala

events.title: NSC-68: Cold War Doctrine Frames LatAm as Battleground
events.detail: NSC-68, the classified 1950 National Security Council report, framed the Cold War as an existential struggle requiring the US to contain Soviet influence globally. This doctrine shaped US policy in Latin America for decades, redefining social reform movements, labor unions, and nationalist governments as potential communist threats requiring US intervention—regardless of their actual ties to the Soviet Union.

sources.name: NSC-68, US National Security Council 1950 (declassified)
Foreign Affairs: Cold War and Latin America

events.title: CIA Established: Institutional Capacity for Covert Action
events.detail: The National Security Act of 1947 created the CIA, giving the US a permanent institutional capacity for covert operations. The CIA's predecessor, the OSS, had operated in Latin America during WWII. Within years, the CIA's Western Hemisphere Division became one of its most active units, overseeing Guatemala (1954), Cuba (1961), and Chile (1973). The CIA's covert action authority derived from a vague NSC directive (NSC 10/2, 1948) that allowed 'propaganda, economic warfare, sabotage, and subversion.'

sources.name: National Security Act of 1947, US Congress
Church Committee Final Report 1976

media.source: Wikipedia / History of the Central Intelligence Agency

events.title: El Bogotazo: Liberal Leader Gaitán Assassinated; Colombia's Violencia Begins
events.detail: The assassination of Colombian Liberal Party leader Jorge Eliécer Gaitán on April 9, 1948 triggered massive urban riots in Bogotá and ignited La Violencia—a decade-long civil conflict killing an estimated 200,000 Colombians. The OAS Ninth International Conference was meeting in Bogotá when riots broke out. Fidel Castro, then a young student activist, was in Bogotá at the time. US policy subsequently supported the Conservative government's counterinsurgency.

sources.name: State Department Colombia cables 1948 (declassified)
NACLA: Colombia Historical Analysis

events.title: Bolivia MNR Revolution: US Initially Opposes, Then Co-opts
events.detail: Bolivia's Nationalist Revolutionary Movement (MNR) overthrew the tin mining oligarchy in April 1952 in a popular revolution. The Eisenhower administration initially considered Operation PBFORTUNE to overthrow the MNR but instead shifted to co-opting it through massive economic aid conditioned on moderate policies. The episode illustrated US flexibility: when outright intervention was too costly, economic leverage was used to shape outcomes.

sources.name: CIA CREST: Bolivia 1952 (declassified)
National Security Archive: Bolivia documents

events.title: Operation PBSUCCESS: CIA Overthrows Árbenz in Guatemala
events.detail: CIA Director Allen Dulles and Secretary of State John Foster Dulles (former United Fruit Company lawyer) orchestrated Operation PBSUCCESS to overthrow elected President Jacobo Árbenz. A CIA-trained mercenary army under Carlos Castillo Armas invaded on June 18, 1954. CIA radio broadcasts (Radio KCALL) created false impressions of military advance. Árbenz resigned on June 27. The coup reversed Guatemala's land reform and ushered in four decades of military rule and genocide.

sources.name: CIA CREST: PBSUCCESS History (declassified)
National Security Archive: Guatemala Declassified

events.title: US Backs Batista Coup in Cuba
events.detail: The US recognized Fulgencio Batista's 1952 military coup within 17 days and continued military and economic support throughout his brutal rule. The Batista government was marked by corruption, gambling (controlled by US mob figures), and political repression. US support continued until Batista's regime became untenable; he fled Cuba on January 1, 1959, as Fidel Castro's forces entered Havana.

sources.name: US State Department Cuba Declassified Files
New York Times Historical Archive 1952–1959

events.title: Cuban Revolution: Castro Takes Havana
events.detail: Fidel Castro's 26th of July Movement defeated Batista's forces and entered Havana on January 8, 1959. The US initially maintained diplomatic relations but grew alarmed by Castro's nationalizations of US-owned properties and alignment with the Soviet Union. CIA planning to overthrow Castro began within months. The US severed diplomatic relations in January 1961.

sources.name: US State Department Cuba Declassified 1959
New York Times January 1959

events.title: US Imposes Trade Embargo on Cuba
events.detail: President Eisenhower imposed a partial trade embargo on Cuba on October 19, 1960, after Cuba nationalized US-owned businesses without compensation. The embargo was expanded under Kennedy in 1962. Now in its seventh decade, the US embargo—which Cuba calls the 'blockade'—has been condemned by overwhelming majorities in UN General Assembly votes annually since 1992.

sources.name: US State Department Cuba Policy Records
UN General Assembly Resolution records

media.source: Wikipedia / United States embargo against Cuba

events.title: Bay of Pigs: CIA-Backed Invasion Fails
events.detail: On April 17, 1961, approximately 1,400 Cuban exiles trained and funded by the CIA (Brigade 2506) landed at Playa Girón (Bay of Pigs) in an attempt to overthrow Castro. President Kennedy withheld promised air support. Within 72 hours, Cuban forces crushed the invasion: 114 brigade members were killed and 1,189 captured. The fiasco was a humiliation for the Kennedy administration and the CIA, and strengthened Castro's position.

sources.name: CIA Inspector General Report on Bay of Pigs (declassified 1998)
National Security Archive: Bay of Pigs

events.title: Operation Mongoose: CIA War Against Cuba
events.detail: Following the Bay of Pigs failure, President Kennedy authorized Operation Mongoose—a covert operation to destabilize Cuba through sabotage, propaganda, and assassination attempts against Castro. The CIA worked with Cuban exile groups and organized crime to attempt Castro's assassination more than 600 times (by Cuban intelligence estimates). Mongoose included attacks on Cuban infrastructure, sugar crops, and factories.

sources.name: Church Committee Report: Alleged Assassination Plots 1975
National Security Archive: Operation Mongoose

events.title: Alliance for Progress: Reform as Anti-Communism
events.detail: President Kennedy launched the Alliance for Progress in March 1961, pledging $20 billion over 10 years in development aid to Latin America. While promoting economic development and social reform, the program explicitly aimed to counter the appeal of communism after the Cuban Revolution. Critics noted that US military aid consistently exceeded economic assistance and often supported repressive regimes.

sources.name: US Department of State: Alliance for Progress Charter 1961
Foreign Affairs: Alliance for Progress Assessment

events.title: Trujillo Assassinated; CIA Provided Weapons
events.detail: Dominican dictator Rafael Trujillo was assassinated on May 30, 1961. Church Committee investigations (1975) confirmed that the CIA had provided weapons to the conspirators, though the extent of direct US operational involvement remains disputed. The assassination removed a US client who had become an embarrassment but left a power vacuum that destabilized the country and contributed to the 1965 US military intervention.

sources.name: Church Committee Final Report: Alleged Assassination Plots 1975
National Security Archive: Dominican Republic

events.title: Cuban Missile Crisis: Brinkmanship Over Cuba
events.detail: In October 1962, US spy planes discovered Soviet nuclear missile installations in Cuba. President Kennedy imposed a naval 'quarantine' and demanded removal of the missiles. For 13 days the world stood at the brink of nuclear war. The crisis was resolved when the Soviet Union agreed to remove missiles from Cuba and the US pledged not to invade Cuba and secretly agreed to remove US missiles from Turkey.

sources.name: Kennedy Library: Cuban Missile Crisis documents
New York Times October 1962

events.title: Brazil Coup: Operation Brother Sam
events.detail: The US supported the military coup that overthrew elected President João Goulart of Brazil on March 31–April 1, 1964. US Ambassador Lincoln Gordon sent a cable to Washington requesting recognition of the new military government before Goulart had even left the country. Operation Brother Sam positioned a US naval task force off the Brazilian coast to support the coup if needed. The military dictatorship that followed lasted 21 years (1964–1985).

sources.name: National Security Archive: Brazil 1964 Declassified
US State Department FOIA: Ambassador Gordon cables

events.title: Dominican Republic: 22,000 US Troops Invade
events.detail: President Lyndon Johnson ordered US Marines and paratroopers into the Dominican Republic on April 28, 1965, citing the threat of a 'communist takeover' after a constitutional revolt sought to restore elected President Juan Bosch. At its peak, 22,000 US troops were deployed (Operation Power Pack). The intervention prevented restoration of the democratically elected government and led to the installation of Joaquín Balaguer, a Trujillo ally.

sources.name: LBJ Library: Dominican Republic cables 1965
New York Times April–September 1965

media.source: Wikipedia / History of the Dominican Republic

events.title: Bolivia: CIA Tracks and Kills Che Guevara
events.detail: CIA officer Félix Rodríguez (alias Max Gomez), working with Bolivian Special Forces trained by US Army Green Berets, tracked and captured Ernesto 'Che' Guevara on October 8, 1967, in La Higuera, Bolivia. Guevara was executed the following day on orders of the Bolivian government, with CIA involvement confirmed by declassified documents. Rodríguez personally interrogated Guevara and photographed his body.

sources.name: CIA CREST: Felix Rodriguez Bolivia 1967 (declassified)
National Security Archive: Che Guevara documents

events.title: Chile: CIA Track I & Track II to Block Allende
events.detail: After Salvador Allende won Chile's 1970 presidential election, the Nixon administration authorized two parallel CIA operations to prevent him from taking office. Track I attempted to convince Chilean Congress to choose runner-up Alessandri. Track II authorized the CIA to instigate a military coup. In service of Track II, the CIA facilitated the assassination attempt on General René Schneider, Army Commander who refused to support a coup.

sources.name: Church Committee Report: Covert Action in Chile 1975
US State Department FOIA Chile Declassified

events.title: Chile: 'Make the Economy Scream'
events.detail: Nixon ordered the CIA to 'make the economy scream' in Chile to destabilize Allende's government. The US orchestrated an 'invisible blockade': cutting off credit lines, pressing international banks to deny loans, and encouraging Chilean truckers to strike. CIA documents show $8 million was allocated to destabilize Chile between 1970–1973. Henry Kissinger authorized covert political operations and media campaigns.

sources.name: Church Committee Report: Covert Action in Chile 1963–1973
National Security Archive: Kissinger Chile cables

events.title: Chile Coup: Pinochet Overthrows Allende; Allende Dies
events.detail: On September 11, 1973, Chilean military forces led by General Augusto Pinochet bombed La Moneda presidential palace and overthrew Salvador Allende. Allende died in the palace—officially by suicide, disputed by some. Pinochet's regime killed at least 3,200 people, tortured 40,000, and forced 200,000 into exile. US involvement was confirmed by declassified CIA documents, though the extent of direct coordination on September 11 remains debated.

sources.name: CIA CREST: Chile coup declassified files
Chilean National Commission on Political Prisoners and Torture (Valech Commission)

events.title: Uruguay: US Backs Military Coup Against Tupamaros
events.detail: The US provided counterinsurgency training and support to Uruguayan security forces targeting the Tupamaro urban guerrilla movement. Dan Mitrione, a USAID Public Safety Program advisor (confirmed CIA contractor), taught torture techniques to Uruguayan police before being kidnapped and killed by Tupamaros in 1970. The military coup of June 1973 established a dictatorship that tortured thousands; Uruguay had the highest per-capita political prisoner rate in the world.

sources.name: US Senate Church Committee: Uruguay documents
Amnesty International: Uruguay Report 1976

events.title: School of the Americas: Training Ground for Repression
events.detail: The US Army's School of the Americas (SOA), established in Panama in 1946 and moved to Fort Benning, Georgia in 1984, trained over 60,000 Latin American military and police officers. Graduates include Noriega (Panama), Ríos Montt (Guatemala), Videla (Argentina), Pinochet associates, and death squad leaders in El Salvador and Honduras. Declassified training manuals (1996) revealed instruction in torture, assassination, and blackmail.

sources.name: US Army SOA Training Manuals (declassified 1996)
School of Americas Watch reports

media.source: Wikipedia / Western Hemisphere Institute for Security Cooperation

events.title: Operation Condor Founded: Transnational State Terror Network
events.detail: Operation Condor was formally established at a November 1975 meeting in Santiago organized by DINA chief Manuel Contreras. The network coordinated intelligence and assassination operations across Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Chile, Paraguay, and Uruguay. Declassified State Department documents confirm US knowledge and logistical support through the CIA and the Panama Canal Zone's communications hub. An estimated 60,000 people were killed and 500 children were stolen.

sources.name: US State Department: Operation Condor declassified cables (Kornbluh collection)
National Security Archive: Operation Condor documents

events.title: Argentina 'Dirty War': US Backs Military Junta
events.detail: The Argentine military overthrew elected President Isabel Perón on March 24, 1976, launching the 'Dirty War' (Guerra Sucia) that disappeared 30,000 people. Declassified documents show Secretary of State Henry Kissinger effectively greenlighted the junta's repression in a June 1976 meeting with Foreign Minister Guzzetti, telling him to 'get it over quickly.' The US maintained military and economic support throughout the junta's rule.

sources.name: US State Department FOIA: Kissinger Argentina cables 1976
Argentine National Commission on Disappeared Persons (CONADEP) Report 1984

events.title: Orlando Letelier Assassinated in Washington DC
events.detail: Former Chilean Foreign Minister Orlando Letelier and his American colleague Ronni Moffitt were killed by a car bomb in Washington DC on September 21, 1976. The assassination was ordered by Pinochet's DINA and carried out by Cuban exile operatives. FBI and CIA investigations were initially impeded by CIA's protection of DINA operative Michael Townley. The incident remains the worst act of foreign-sponsored terrorism on US soil prior to 9/11.

sources.name: FBI: Letelier-Moffitt assassination investigation files (declassified)
National Security Archive: Letelier documents

events.title: Condor Phase Three: International Assassination Campaign
events.detail: Operation Condor's Phase Three involved assassinations of exiled opposition leaders in Europe, the US, and elsewhere. Targets included Bernardo Leighton (shot in Rome, 1975), Juan José Torres (killed in Buenos Aires, 1976), and Letelier in Washington. The US provided communications infrastructure via the Panama Canal Zone's Condortel system, according to declassified cables.

sources.name: US State Department: Condor declassified cables (Martin Edwin Andersen)
Human Rights Watch: Operation Condor Report

events.title: Carter Human Rights Policy: A Partial Shift
events.detail: President Jimmy Carter made human rights central to US foreign policy, cutting military aid to Argentina, Uruguay, and El Salvador based on human rights violations. He signed the Panama Canal Treaties in 1977, transferring sovereignty to Panama. However, Carter's approach was inconsistent: he maintained close ties with pro-US dictators (the Shah, Somoza initially) and did not fundamentally alter US policy toward Operation Condor countries.

sources.name: Carter Library: Human Rights Policy records
Foreign Affairs: Carter Human Rights

events.title: Sandinista Revolution Overthrows Somoza
events.detail: The Sandinista National Liberation Front (FSLN) overthrew Anastasio Somoza Debayle's dictatorship on July 17–19, 1979. The Carter administration had cut off aid to Somoza after 50 years of US support for the Somoza family dynasty. Somoza fled to Paraguay, where he was assassinated in September 1980. The Sandinista government would become the target of a massive CIA covert war under Reagan.

sources.name: Carter Library: Nicaragua declassified 1979
National Security Archive: Nicaragua Revolution

events.title: Archbishop Romero Assassinated; US Continues El Salvador Aid
events.detail: Archbishop Óscar Romero of El Salvador was shot dead while saying Mass on March 24, 1980, by a gunman linked to right-wing death squads. The UN Truth Commission (1993) found that death squad organizer Roberto D'Aubuisson ordered the assassination. The US continued to provide military aid to El Salvador's government despite the murder and the December 1980 rape-murder of four US churchwomen by Salvadoran security forces.

sources.name: UN El Salvador Truth Commission Report: From Madness to Hope 1993
Americas Watch (HRW): El Salvador reports

events.title: DINA Established: Chile's Secret Terror Apparatus
events.detail: Pinochet formally established the Dirección de Inteligencia Nacional (DINA) under Manuel Contreras in June 1974. DINA ran Villa Grimaldi and other torture centers, coordinated international assassinations (including Letelier in Washington DC in 1976), and was the primary institutional mechanism of Pinochet's terror. CIA maintained regular contact with DINA; CIA Inspector General later confirmed Contreras was a paid CIA asset.

sources.name: Chilean Valech Commission Report 2004
CIA IG Report: Contreras relationship (declassified)

events.title: Cubana Flight 455: CIA-Trained Exiles Bomb Cuban Airliner
events.detail: CIA-trained Cuban exile Luis Posada Carriles orchestrated the bombing of Cubana de Aviación Flight 455 on October 6, 1976, killing all 73 people aboard—including Cuba's national fencing team. Posada escaped from Venezuelan prison in 1985 with alleged CIA help. Despite Venezuelan and Cuban extradition requests, Posada lived freely in Miami until his 2018 death—representing one of the starkest examples of selective US counterterrorism policy.

sources.name: FBI: Cubana Flight 455 investigation files (declassified)
National Security Archive: Posada Carriles documents

events.title: Reagan Authorizes Contra War Against Nicaragua
events.detail: President Reagan signed National Security Decision Directive 17 in November 1981, authorizing the CIA to fund, arm, and train Nicaraguan Contra rebels with $19 million. The Contras operated primarily from Honduras and Costa Rica, conducting raids into Nicaragua. CIA Director William Casey called the Contras 'freedom fighters.' By 1983, Congress grew alarmed; the Boland Amendment (1984) banned US government assistance to the Contras.

sources.name: NSDD-17: Presidential Finding on Nicaragua, US National Archives (declassified)
National Security Archive: Contra files

events.title: El Mozote Massacre: US-Trained Battalion Kills 1,000 Civilians
events.detail: The Atlacatl Battalion—El Salvador's first rapid deployment unit, trained at the US Army's School of the Americas—massacred approximately 1,000 civilians (including 553 children) in the village of El Mozote and surrounding hamlets on December 11–13, 1981. US Ambassador Deane Hinton and the Reagan administration initially denied and covered up the massacre. The UN Truth Commission later confirmed it was the largest massacre in modern Latin American history.

sources.name: UN El Salvador Truth Commission: El Mozote 1993
New York Times: Raymond Bonner reporting December 1981

events.title: CIA Mines Nicaraguan Harbors: ICJ Rules Against US
events.detail: The CIA secretly mined Nicaraguan harbors in early 1984 without informing Congress, damaging vessels from the Netherlands, Japan, the Soviet Union, and Panama. When revealed, it caused a major scandal; Congress erupted. Nicaragua filed suit at the International Court of Justice, which ruled in June 1986 that the US had violated international law and owed Nicaragua reparations. The US rejected the court's jurisdiction.

sources.name: ICJ: Nicaragua v. United States, 1986 judgment
Wall Street Journal: CIA mining investigation 1984

events.title: Operation Urgent Fury: US Invades Grenada
events.detail: The US invaded Grenada on October 25, 1983, just days after the murder of Prime Minister Maurice Bishop in an internal coup. Reagan cited protection of American medical students as justification, though critics called it primarily an effort to prevent a Marxist government from consolidating power. Around 7,000 US troops participated. The UN General Assembly passed a resolution calling the invasion a 'flagrant violation of international law' by a vote of 108–9.

sources.name: US Department of Defense: Operation Urgent Fury after-action report
UN General Assembly Resolution A/38/7 1983

media.source: Wikipedia / United States invasion of Grenada

events.title: Iran-Contra Affair: Illegal Arms-for-Hostages-for-Contras
events.detail: In November 1986, it was revealed that senior Reagan administration officials had secretly sold arms to Iran (despite an embargo) and illegally diverted the proceeds to fund the Contras, circumventing the Boland Amendment. National Security Council staff Oliver North ran the scheme. The Tower Commission and Congressional investigations found Reagan had been 'out of the loop' on specifics but created the environment enabling the operation. No senior official was ultimately imprisoned.

sources.name: Tower Commission Report 1987
Iran-Contra Independent Counsel Final Report 1993

events.title: Honduras: Transformed into US Military Platform
events.detail: The Reagan administration turned Honduras into a strategic base for operations against Nicaragua and El Salvador, building or upgrading military facilities and increasing US military presence from 25 to 1,200 soldiers. The CIA built a secret Contra training base at Las Vegas near the Nicaraguan border. General Gustavo Álvarez Martínez, armed with US support, ran Battalion 3-16, a secret death squad that disappeared 184 suspected subversives.

sources.name: US General Accounting Office: Honduras Military Aid report 1988
National Security Archive: Honduras Battalion 3-16

events.title: Guatemala: Ríos Montt Genocide with US Backing
events.detail: US-trained general Efraín Ríos Montt came to power in a coup in March 1982 and launched 'scorched earth' campaigns against indigenous Maya communities suspected of supporting guerrillas. An estimated 75,000 civilians were killed during 1981–1983. Reagan called Ríos Montt 'a man of great personal integrity' and restored military aid. In 2013, a Guatemalan court convicted Ríos Montt of genocide—the first such conviction of a head of state by his own country.

sources.name: Guatemala Historical Clarification Commission (CEH) Report 1999
Guatemala genocide trial judgment 2013

events.title: Kerry Committee: CIA Knew of Contra Drug Trafficking
events.detail: A Senate investigation led by John Kerry (1987–1989) found that the CIA and other US agencies had knowledge of, and in some cases facilitated, drug trafficking by Contra groups and their supporters. CIA-connected drug traffickers flew drugs into the US and returned with weapons for the Contras. The Kerry Committee concluded that 'US government officials involved in Contra supply networks... failed to address drug trafficking because they did not want to jeopardize the war effort against Nicaragua.'

sources.name: Kerry Committee Report: Drugs, Law Enforcement and Foreign Policy 1989
CIA Inspector General Report on Contra drug trafficking 1998

events.title: Four US Churchwomen Murdered by Salvadoran Security Forces
events.detail: Maryknoll Sisters Ita Ford and Maura Clarke, Ursuline Sister Dorothy Kazel, and lay missionary Jean Donovan were raped and murdered by Salvadoran National Guard members on December 2, 1980. The Reagan transition team—and later Reagan officials—attempted to suggest the women were 'running guns' to guerrillas. The killers were eventually convicted in 1984; evidence linked the order to senior military figures never prosecuted.

sources.name: UN El Salvador Truth Commission: Churchwomen case 1993
Americas Watch: El Salvador churchwomen investigation

events.title: Boland Amendment: Congress Bans Contra Aid; Reagan Circumvents It
events.detail: Congress passed the Boland Amendment in October 1984, prohibiting the CIA, DoD, and intelligence agencies from directly or indirectly supporting the Contras. Rather than complying, the Reagan administration circumvented the law through Oliver North's private 'Enterprise' network, using Iranian arms proceeds and foreign donations. The Iran-Contra scandal emerged from this systematic violation of Congressional authority.

sources.name: Boland Amendment to DoD Appropriations Act 1984, US Congress
Iran-Contra Report: Congressional committees 1987

events.title: Esquipulas II: Central American Leaders Make Peace Over US Objections
events.detail: Five Central American presidents signed the Esquipulas II Peace Accords on August 7, 1987, providing a framework for ending regional conflicts including the Contra war. Costa Rican President Oscar Arias won the 1987 Nobel Peace Prize for brokering the deal. The Reagan administration opposed the accords and continued supporting the Contras. The accords eventually led to the 1990 Nicaraguan elections.

sources.name: Esquipulas II Procedure for the Establishment of a Firm and Lasting Peace in Central America 1987
Nobel Committee: Arias Prize citation 1987

events.title: Operation Just Cause: US Invades Panama
events.detail: The US launched Operation Just Cause on December 20, 1989, with 27,000 troops to capture General Manuel Noriega (a former CIA asset turned drug trafficker). Noriega surrendered on January 3, 1990. The US-acknowledged civilian death toll was 202–300 Panamanians; independent investigators (Americas Watch, Ramsey Clark investigation) estimated 300–1,000+ civilian deaths, with entire neighborhoods destroyed. The UN General Assembly condemned the invasion 75–20.

sources.name: US Southern Command: Operation Just Cause After-Action Report
Americas Watch: The Laws of War and the Conduct of the Panama Invasion 1990

media.source: Wikipedia / United States invasion of Panama

events.title: Nicaragua Elections: Sandinistas Defeated, Contras Disband
events.detail: In February 1990, Violeta Chamorro defeated Daniel Ortega in internationally observed elections, ending the Sandinista government. The Contras agreed to demobilize under the Esquipulas peace process. The Contra war had cost approximately 30,000 lives and billions in economic damage. Nicaragua, once the second poorest nation in the hemisphere, struggled to rebuild under IMF-mandated structural adjustment programs.

sources.name: OAS Electoral Observation Mission: Nicaragua 1990
National Security Archive: Contra War end

events.title: Haiti: Military Coup Ousts Aristide; CIA Link
events.detail: Haitian General Raoul Cédras overthrew President Jean-Bertrand Aristide—Haiti's first democratically elected president—on September 30, 1991. Subsequent investigations revealed that Emmanuel Constant, founder of the FRAPH paramilitary group that terrorized Haiti under the junta, was on the CIA payroll. The US imposed economic sanctions on Haiti but was slow to act against the coup leaders.

sources.name: US Congressional testimony: CIA-FRAPH connection 1994
New York Times: Constant-CIA connection investigation

events.title: Operation Uphold Democracy: Haiti Intervention
events.detail: After three years of junta rule and brutal repression by FRAPH paramilitaries, President Clinton authorized Operation Uphold Democracy in September 1994. Former President Carter negotiated a last-minute agreement causing the junta to stand down. US and multinational forces entered Haiti peacefully, restoring Aristide. The intervention was widely supported but critics noted it did little to address Haiti's structural poverty and US corporate interests.

sources.name: US Southern Command: Operation Uphold Democracy report
UN Security Council Resolution 940 (1994)

events.title: El Salvador Peace Accords End 12-Year Civil War
events.detail: The Chapultepec Peace Accords were signed on January 16, 1992, ending El Salvador's civil war, which had killed 75,000 people over 12 years. The UN Truth Commission (1993) attributed 85% of human rights violations to government forces and US-backed death squads. The accords required dissolution of the Atlacatl Battalion and other units with human rights records. The US had provided $6 billion in military and economic aid to El Salvador during the conflict.

sources.name: UN El Salvador Truth Commission Report 1993
Congressional Research Service: El Salvador aid totals

events.title: Plan Colombia: $1.3 Billion for War on Drugs
events.detail: President Clinton signed Plan Colombia in July 2000, committing $1.3 billion in US military and police assistance—the largest US security aid package since Camp David—ostensibly to fight drug trafficking. Critics noted 75% was military aid and that the plan enabled Colombian security forces to fight FARC guerrillas while fumigating thousands of hectares of crops affecting indigenous communities. Plan Colombia was expanded under Bush into direct counterinsurgency operations.

sources.name: US State Department: Plan Colombia program documents
Latin American Working Group: Plan Colombia critique

events.title: NAFTA: Economic Intervention and Zapatista Uprising
events.detail: NAFTA took effect January 1, 1994—the same day the Zapatista Army of National Liberation (EZLN) launched an uprising in Chiapas, Mexico, declaring NAFTA a 'death sentence' for indigenous communities. NAFTA's agricultural provisions devastated small Mexican farmers unable to compete with US subsidized corn, contributing to mass migration north. NAFTA also required Mexico to privatize state industries and liberalize capital flows.

sources.name: US Trade Representative: NAFTA text 1993
Foreign Affairs: NAFTA and Mexico impact

events.title: Church Committee: Senate Exposes CIA Assassination Plots
events.detail: The Senate Select Committee to Study Governmental Operations with Respect to Intelligence Activities (the Church Committee), chaired by Senator Frank Church, conducted landmark hearings in 1975 exposing CIA assassination plots against foreign leaders including Patrice Lumumba, Fidel Castro, and Rafael Trujillo. The committee also documented covert action against Allende's Chile, NSA mass surveillance, and FBI COINTELPRO. Its findings led to creation of permanent Congressional oversight of intelligence agencies.

sources.name: Church Committee Final Report: Alleged Assassination Plots Involving Foreign Leaders 1975
Church Committee: Covert Action in Chile 1963–1973

media.source: Wikipedia / John F. Kennedy assassination conspiracy theories

events.title: Pinochet Arrested in London: International Justice Reaches Dictators
events.detail: Former Chilean dictator Augusto Pinochet was arrested in London on October 16, 1998, based on a Spanish extradition request for torture and crimes against humanity. The arrest was a watershed moment for international human rights law. Pinochet was ultimately returned to Chile in 2000 on health grounds. He died in 2006 under house arrest facing 300 criminal charges. The arrest prompted accelerated declassification of US Chile documents.

sources.name: UK House of Lords: R v Bow Street Metropolitan Stipendiary Magistrate (1999)
Amnesty International: Pinochet arrest statement 1998

events.title: Venezuela: US-Backed Coup Attempt Against Chávez
events.detail: Venezuelan President Hugo Chávez was briefly overthrown on April 11, 2002, following a military coup and business lockout. Pedro Carmona, head of the Venezuelan business federation (FEDECAMARAS), was installed as president. The Bush administration recognized Carmona and praised the 'democratic' change of power. Chávez was restored to power within 47 hours after mass protests. Declassified US documents show the State Department and NED had funded opposition groups.

sources.name: US State Department cables: Venezuela 2002 (declassified)
National Endowment for Democracy: Venezuela grants 2001–2002

events.title: Haiti: Aristide Removed in US-Facilitated Coup
events.detail: On February 29, 2004, US Marines and diplomatic personnel pressured President Aristide to leave Haiti as armed rebels (including former FRAPH members) advanced on Port-au-Prince. Aristide later claimed he was kidnapped and forced onto a US plane to the Central African Republic. The US, France, and Canada denied this account. The US deployed 2,000 troops and helped install an interim government, effectively ending Aristide's presidency.

sources.name: Aristide statement to journalists: 'I was kidnapped' 2004
New York Times: Aristide removal coverage February 2004

events.title: Honduras Coup: Zelaya Ousted in Pajamas
events.detail: Honduran President Manuel Zelaya was seized by the military at gunpoint on June 28, 2009, and flown to Costa Rica in his pajamas. The coup followed Zelaya's plans for a non-binding poll on convening a constitutional assembly—opposed by the business community and military. The Obama administration initially called it a coup, but later Secretary Clinton worked to facilitate elections that legitimized the new government rather than restoring Zelaya. US military aid to Honduras continued throughout.

sources.name: Clinton, Hillary: Hard Choices (memoir) — acknowledges managing transition
National Security Archive: Honduras 2009 cables

events.title: Obama-Castro: Historic Cuba Normalization Announced
events.detail: Presidents Obama and Raúl Castro simultaneously announced on December 17, 2014, that the US and Cuba would restore diplomatic relations after 53 years of estrangement. The agreement included prisoner exchanges, easing of travel restrictions, and the removal of Cuba from the US State Sponsors of Terrorism list. The US embassy in Havana reopened in August 2015. Obama visited Cuba in March 2016—the first US president to visit since Calvin Coolidge in 1928.

sources.name: White House press release: US-Cuba normalization December 17, 2014
New York Times: Cuba normalization coverage

media.source: Wikipedia / United States embargo against Cuba

events.title: Trump Reverses Cuba Normalization; Tightens Embargo
events.detail: President Trump announced in June 2017 that he would partially reverse Obama's normalization with Cuba, reinstituting travel restrictions and banning commercial dealings with Cuban military-linked entities. In 2021, Biden designated Cuba a State Sponsor of Terrorism—a designation Cuba denounced as politically motivated. The embargo remains the world's longest-standing trade embargo.

sources.name: White House: Cuba Policy directive June 2017
Reuters: Cuba sanctions tightening

media.source: Wikipedia / United States embargo against Cuba

events.title: US Recognizes Guaidó as Venezuela 'President'
events.detail: The Trump administration recognized National Assembly president Juan Guaidó as Venezuela's 'interim president' on January 23, 2019, following Guaidó's self-proclamation in a public square. The US imposed severe economic sanctions, froze Venezuelan assets, and transferred Venezuelan gold reserves and PDVSA oil assets to Guaidó's control. The coup attempt failed; Maduro retained power. Guaidó's government was recognized by 50+ countries but never controlled Venezuelan territory.

sources.name: US Treasury OFAC: Venezuela sanctions orders 2019
Reuters: Venezuela sanctions economic impact

events.title: Bolivia: Evo Morales Forced Out After Election Controversy
events.detail: Bolivian President Evo Morales resigned on November 10, 2019, after the Organization of American States (OAS) claimed to find 'clear manipulation' in the October 27 election results. The military and police withdrew support under pressure. A Center for Economic and Policy Research (CEPR) analysis found statistical errors in the OAS report, concluding the original results may have been valid. The Trump administration praised Morales's removal; Bolivia's interim government received swift US recognition.

sources.name: OAS Electoral Integrity Mission: Bolivia 2019 preliminary report
CEPR: Analysis of the OAS Bolivia report 2020

events.title: Venezuela Sanctions: Humanitarian Crisis Deepens
events.detail: Escalating US sanctions on Venezuela—particularly the 2019 Executive Order blocking virtually all economic transactions—have contributed to a severe humanitarian crisis. A UN Human Rights Council Fact-Finding Mission (2020) documented healthcare system collapse, food insecurity, and emigration of over 5.4 million Venezuelans. UN Special Rapporteur Alena Douhan (2021) concluded US sanctions violated human rights and international law.

sources.name: UN Human Rights Council FFM Venezuela 2020
UN Special Rapporteur Venezuela sanctions report 2021

events.title: Declassification Era: CIA Confirms Historical Coups
events.detail: Beginning in the 1990s, US agencies declassified thousands of documents confirming long-suspected CIA interventions. Key releases: 1997–CIA Guatemala declassification; 1999–Pinochet arrests prompt State Dept Chile document release (1,100 documents); 2003–CIA releases Guatemala PBSUCCESS history; 2016–Obama opens CIA files on Argentina dirty war. These documents validated accounts of survivors and historians and enabled accountability proceedings.

sources.name: CIA CREST database (declassified)
National Security Archive: US declassification campaigns

events.title: Nicaragua: Ortega's Authoritarian Turn; US Sanctions
events.detail: Former Sandinista guerrilla Daniel Ortega—who returned to the presidency in 2007—cracked down violently on student protests in April 2018, killing 325+ people. In 2021, Ortega imprisoned all major opposition presidential candidates before elections and expelled foreign NGOs. The US imposed multiple rounds of sanctions. The country that was once the target of US destabilization is now governed by an authoritarian government that the US condemns.

sources.name: Inter-American Commission on Human Rights: Nicaragua 2018 report
US Treasury OFAC: Nicaragua NICA Act sanctions

events.title: El Salvador: Bukele's State of Exception — New Model of Authoritarianism
events.detail: President Nayib Bukele declared a State of Exception in March 2022, suspending due process rights and mass-arresting over 70,000 people in an anti-gang crackdown. Bukele—who has adopted authoritarian methods and Bitcoin as legal tender—received tacit US support as gang violence fell, despite human rights organizations documenting torture and arbitrary detention. The Biden administration certified El Salvador for some aid while raising concerns.

sources.name: Human Rights Watch: El Salvador State of Exception report 2022
US State Department: El Salvador human rights certification 2022

events.title: Haiti Gang Crisis: US Backs Kenyan Intervention Amid Continuing Instability
events.detail: Haiti's governance collapsed in 2024 as armed gangs controlled 80% of Port-au-Prince. Following the 2021 assassination of President Jovenel Moïse, the US backed a Kenyan-led multinational security force to restore order. Critics noted that decades of US intervention—including backing the 1991 and 2004 coups and supporting post-earthquake reconstruction that enriched contractors—contributed structurally to Haiti's instability.

sources.name: UN Security Council Resolution 2699 (2023): Haiti multinational force
OHCHR: Haiti human rights situation 2024

events.title: Pink Tide: Latin America Shifts Left; US Responds with Pressure
events.detail: The early 2000s saw a wave of left-wing governments elected across Latin America—Lula (Brazil), Kirchner (Argentina), Morales (Bolivia), Correa (Ecuador), Chávez (Venezuela), Ortega (Nicaragua), Lugo (Paraguay), Mujica (Uruguay). The US responded with economic pressure, support for opposition movements, and strategic relationships with right-wing successor governments. The Pink Tide began receding after 2015.

sources.name: Foreign Affairs: Latin America's Left Turn 2006
National Security Archive: NED Latin America grants 2004–2012

events.title: Operation Absolute Resolve: US Military Captures Venezuelan President Maduro
events.detail: On January 3, 2026, US Special Operations forces launched Operation Absolute Resolve against Venezuela. Over 150 aircraft participated in strikes disabling Venezuelan air defenses before a Delta Force team raided President Nicolás Maduro's compound in Caracas. Maduro and his wife Cilia Flores were captured and flown to New York to face federal charges of narco-terrorism, cocaine trafficking, and weapons offenses. The Venezuelan Interior Ministry reported over 100 killed; seven US service members were injured. The operation—the most direct US military intervention in Latin America since the 1989 Panama invasion—was condemned by Brazil, Chile, Colombia, Mexico, Uruguay, and Spain as a violation of the UN Charter. The Trump administration characterized Maduro as a 'narco-terrorist' whose removal was necessary to protect US national security.

sources.name: CBS News: US Military Strikes — Maduro Capture
CNBC: US-Venezuela Military Operation — Casualties
Washington Post: Maduro Capture

media.caption: US Special Operations capture Venezuelan President Maduro in Operation Absolute Resolve, January 3, 2026

events.title: Trump's EO 14380: First Naval Blockade of Cuba Since the Missile Crisis
events.detail: President Trump signed Executive Order 14380 on January 29, 2026, declaring a national emergency and authorizing tariffs on any country supplying oil to Cuba—effectively the first US naval blockade of Cuba since the 1962 Cuban Missile Crisis. Tankers from Mexico's Pemex were intercepted. UN experts condemned the order as a human rights violation. By February, fuel shortages had halted garbage collection in Havana. Cuba entered diplomatic talks with the US under duress, while Russia attempted to send oil tankers in late March, which OFAC also blocked.

sources.name: OHCHR: UN Experts Condemn US Fuel Blockade on Cuba
HK Law: EO 14380 — Tariffs on Countries Supplying Cuba
Al Jazeera: Russia Sends Second Ship to Cuba Amid US Blockade

media.caption: UN experts condemn Trump's Cuba oil blockade as a human rights violation

events.title: Operation Southern Spear & Expanded US Military Presence in Latin America
events.detail: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth launched Operation Southern Spear in November 2025, deploying the largest US naval fleet to the Caribbean in decades—4,500+ sailors and Marines—targeting drug trafficking with autonomous interdiction systems. By early 2026, the operation had reported 45+ vessel strikes. In March 2026, the Pentagon announced US Special Forces embedded with Ecuadorian commandos for joint anti-cartel raids on Ecuadorian soil. On March 19, 2026, Pentagon officials warned of a potential deployment of up to 15,000 additional ground troops across Latin America—which would constitute the largest US military presence in the region since the Cold War.

sources.name: Wikipedia: US Military Buildup in the Caribbean — Operation Southern Spear
Al Jazeera: Trump Administration Launches US Military Operation in Ecuador
Colombia One: US Ground Troop Deployment in LatAm

media.caption: US Special Forces launch operations in Ecuador as part of expanded Latin American military presence, March 2026
